slides.title: Implicit Fulfillment 🥷
slides.content:
## No 'Implements' Keyword

In Java or C#, if a `Dog` wants to fulfill an `Animal` interface, you actively have to type `class Dog implements Animal`.

Go is different. In Go, interfaces are fulfilled **implicitly**. If your struct simply has all the methods listed in the interface, Go automatically considers it a match! You don't have to announce it.

slides.title: Putting it Together 🧩
slides.content: Let's see the `Speaker` interface in action perfectly linking two entirely different structs!

quizData.question: How do you explicitly tell Go that a `Car` struct implements a `Vehicle` interface?

options.text: You don't. You simply give the Car struct the methods required by Vehicle.

quizData.explanation: Implicit interfaces are a hallmark of Go. If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, Go proudly considers it a duck natively.
